source_cards.spark: Mathematical invention works by merging two incomplete theories — and it is precisely their incompleteness that makes the merge productive. Your system is built on the same principle: the half-formed concept is not a problem to fix before the work begins, it is the mechanism by which the work begins.
source_cards.bridge: The anchor's soft-anchor mechanism and Bou et al.'s generic space are the same structural object: something rich enough to recognize that two things belong together, but empty enough not to predetermine what emerges from that recognition. In both cases, the anchor/generic-space does not say 'go here' — it says 'these two things are in structural conversation, now run the blend and see what falls out.' The paper's operationalization of incompleteness-as-match-surface maps directly onto the anchor's treatment of the user's half-formed concept: a fully articulated, closed concept would be like a fully axiomatized theory — nothing new can emerge because there is no open surface for incoming structure to attach to. The user's vagueness is not noise to be cleaned before the system runs; it is the exact feature that makes resonance possible. Where the paper uses iterative inconsistency-resolution to crystallize the blend into a stable new theory, your system's process of surfacing connections and letting the user respond is doing the same work — the card the user engages with is the blend being 'run,' and the user's reaction is the inconsistency-resolution step that tightens or weakens the emerging structure.
source_cards.source_shape: Bou et al. model mathematical discovery as conceptual blending: two partial theories — say, a fragment of natural number arithmetic and a fragment of functional analysis — are fed into a formal process that finds their shared skeleton (the 'generic space') and constructs a new theory, called the colimit, that inherits structure from both without being identical to either. The key move is that neither input theory is complete. Full, closed theories have no room left for emergent structure — every consequence is already derivable. It is the gaps, the missing axioms, the unresolved tensions between the two inputs, that the blending process fills with something genuinely new. The system then 'runs' the blend: it derives theorems that were not present in either input, discovers inconsistencies, weakens axioms to resolve them, and iterates.
source_cards.use: Consider framing the user's concept not as an input to be clarified before the system works, but as a deliberately partial axiomatization — and design the card-response loop explicitly as a blending iteration: each card the user engages with adds a constraint that tightens the generic space, and each card they reject weakens an implicit axiom, until the colimit (their advanced concept) crystallizes from the accumulated resolutions.
source_cards.limit: The blending process in Bou et al. is fully deterministic and formally verifiable: given the two input spaces and the morphisms connecting them to the generic space, the colimit is uniquely computed and its consistency is checkable by machine. That requires the human to name both input theories and identify the generic space before the machinery runs — the entire structure is drawn in advance, then executed. Your system's premise is the opposite: the user cannot and should not name those spaces, because the wandering agents are what surface them. There is also no counterpart in the paper to the retrospective-path-as-coherent-map: in conceptual blending, the diagram precedes the blend; in your system, the only coherent account of why a path was productive emerges after the path is walked. The bridge holds tightly for the incompleteness-as-productive-surface and generic-space-as-soft-anchor shapes, but breaks entirely at the question of who specifies the morphisms and when.

source_cards.spark: A reinforcement learning system built to discover new mathematics invented a scoring function that recognizes valuable territory without ever specifying a destination — and that mechanism is structurally identical to how your anchor is supposed to work.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor plays the same role as FERMAT's interestingness function: it is a context that scores encountered material without directing the path. The agent doesn't ask 'is this the destination?' — it asks 'does this resonate with what I'm already carrying?' — which is exactly how soft anchoring is supposed to work. The MDP's sparse reward structure maps onto the past-trackable/future-unpredictable asymmetry: the only coherent account of where the agent has been is the retrospective trajectory, and no forward map exists before the traversal happens. And FERMAT's incompleteness is load-bearing in the same way yours is — a fully specified target would collapse the search into retrieval, not discovery; the partial skeleton is what keeps the match surface large enough to catch something surprising.
source_cards.source_shape: FERMAT treats mathematical theory formation as a Markov Decision Process: an agent moves through a vast space of possible mathematical objects, and at each step it scores what it finds using a learned 'interestingness' function I(m, S) — a heuristic that reads the current discovered set S and asks whether the new object m is worth keeping, without ever being told where to go next. The reward signal is sparse, meaning the agent receives almost no feedback during traversal and only occasionally encounters something that scores high. Over time, high-scoring subroutines get extracted and reused as scaffolding through a mechanism called EvoAbstract, compounding earlier finds into reusable structure rather than starting from scratch each time.
source_cards.use: Consider whether FERMAT's interestingness function — score against current-state context, not against a fixed target — gives you a concrete operational template for implementing the soft anchor's scoring behavior: not 'does this match the anchor's content?' but 'does this shift the anchor's state in a way that feels generative?'
source_cards.limit: FERMAT's interestingness function is trained against a ground-truth set of known mathematical entities — the reward fires when the agent rediscovers something already validated by human mathematicians. That means FERMAT is finding an open path toward a known answer. Your system requires an open path toward an unknown answer, where the anchor is a partial skeleton with no fixed destination and no pre-validated target set. If you tried to score resonance against a fixed reference the way FERMAT does, you would destroy the vagueness that makes the anchor useful — the system would converge on what is already known rather than surface what the user hasn't seen yet. The bridge holds for the scoring-without-directing shape; it breaks completely at the question of what the score is actually measured against.

source_cards.spark: Mathematical creativity researchers discovered that two ideas can only truly fuse when both are *incomplete* — full, rigid structures don't blend, they collide. Your system's reliance on partial, underspecified anchors isn't a weakness in the design; it's the exact condition that makes generative connection possible.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor is the first input space, and it works precisely because it is underspecified — a skeleton, not a finished structure. The wandering agent's job is to find a second input space that shares enough abstract structure with the anchor to be blend-compatible, which is exactly what the generic space detects in formal blending theory. The agent's 'soft anchor scores without directing' mechanism maps directly onto the generic space's role: it doesn't dictate what the match must look like, it only registers when a structural kinship is present. And the anchor's principle that incompleteness is maximum match surface is not a metaphor here — blending theory formalizes the same claim: a fully articulated anchor would reduce the match surface to near-zero, because rigid structures can only collide with other rigid structures, not fuse with them.
source_cards.source_shape: Conceptual blending theory holds that new ideas emerge when two partial 'input spaces' — neither fully specified nor fully compatible with each other — are mapped onto a shared abstract skeleton called the 'generic space.' The generic space doesn't tell you what the blend will look like; it only tells you that the two inputs are compatible enough to be fused. The emergent blend is genuinely new: it isn't contained in either input, and it couldn't have been predicted from either alone. Crucially, the theory shows that completeness is the enemy of blending — the more rigidly defined an input space is, the less surface it offers for a productive fusion.
source_cards.use: Consider framing the anchor's incompleteness not as a provisional state to be resolved, but as a permanent design parameter — the system might actively resist over-specification of the anchor, treating any attempt to 'complete' it as a signal that the match surface is shrinking. You could even surface this to users: when their anchor feels too vague to work with, that vagueness may be exactly what makes the system's connections land.
source_cards.limit: Blending theory is a dyadic operation: it requires two named input spaces that are both present before the blend can be analyzed. The formalism models the fusion event itself with precision, but it has nothing to say about how you find the second input space in the first place — the search phase, the wander, the traversal that ends when something blend-compatible is discovered, is entirely outside its scope. This means blending theory can validate and describe what happens when your agent finds a match, but it cannot model or guide the wandering that precedes the match. The bridge holds for the 'why incompleteness works' and 'what a soft anchor is doing' questions; it breaks the moment you ask it to explain how the agent should move through the space before it arrives anywhere.

selection.why_these_cards: Conceptual blending (P02-002) gives the formal colimit machinery but requires both inputs and the generic space named in advance. FERMAT (P04-002) provides a scoring function that emerges from search and needs no pre-named target. S0b2583 sharpens the claim that completeness is the enemy of fusion. The sharpest tension is between P02-002's pre-drawn diagram and FERMAT's path-discovered scorer — both blend, but one specifies before, one discovers during.
selection.spark: Blending theory says the colimit is uniquely computed once you draw the morphisms in advance; FERMAT says the scoring function should NOT exist in advance or it collapses into retrieval. Collide them: what if the morphisms themselves are what the wander synthesizes?
selection.motive: The cushion's hardest problem is that the user cannot name the second input space (the prior work to inherit from) — they only have a vague anchor. This blend produces the machinery for the system to construct the morphism as it wanders, not before.
selection.tension: P02-002 requires the generic space drawn before execution; P04-002 forbids the scorer existing before execution. One is a feed-forward diagram, the other is a self-modifying search. They cannot both be true of the same system unless something new mediates them.

blends.thesis: A morphism-synthesizing wander: the structural mapping between the user's anchor and a found prior work is not pre-drawn (as blending requires) nor frozen (as a static scorer would be) — it is incrementally constructed by the act of scoring resonance, so that each accepted find tightens an emerging generic space that did not exist when the wander began.
blends.mechanism: The agent treats the anchor as input-space L with no R and no G specified. As it traverses, each candidate is scored not by 'does this match the anchor' but by 'does this candidate let me draw a coherent partial morphism between itself and the anchor.' A successful partial morphism IS the generic space, discovered retrospectively. Accepted cards add morphism edges; rejected cards delete provisional edges. The colimit (the advanced concept) crystallizes as the morphism set stabilizes — the diagram is the output, not the input.
blends.emergent_structure: The generic space is constructed BY the scoring act rather than supplied to it — neither card allows this. Blending theory hands the generic space to the algorithm; FERMAT scores against discovered objects but never builds the mapping between domains. The blend makes the mapping itself the thing the scorer produces.
blends.advances_cushion: Solves the core 'who names the second input and when' problem directly: the user supplies only the vague anchor, and the system manufactures the inheritance relationship to the prior work as a byproduct of wandering — exactly the 'take a published method to the next level without already knowing how' case the user described.

source_cards.spark: A 1960s learning theory encodes partial knowledge as a binary vector moving up a dependency tree — and that partial state turns out to be a precise diagnostic surface, not a defect. Your system treats vagueness the same way: incompleteness is the map.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's anchor works as a partial state too — a user's concept or work-in-progress is never fully specified, and that vagueness has been a design tension. Gagné's encoding suggests a reframe: the partial state isn't noise to be resolved before the system can act, it's a recognition signature that locates the user in a space of possible directions. Where Gagné uses the gap to identify which prerequisite is missing, your system could use the gap to identify which structural resonances are most likely to be generative — the incompleteness points toward what's needed rather than merely marking what's absent. The key correspondence is that both systems treat a partial encoding as load-bearing information, not as a placeholder for the complete picture that hasn't arrived yet.
source_cards.source_shape: Gagné's inheritance coding works by representing a learner's knowledge state as a binary vector across a prerequisite hierarchy: each skill is either mastered or not, and a higher skill is only reachable if every skill below it in the chain is already marked complete. The system can therefore look at any partial vector and immediately locate two things at once — where the learner currently stands, and exactly which gap is blocking forward movement. The hierarchy doesn't just track progress; it converts incompleteness into a precise diagnostic address.
source_cards.use: Consider representing a user's anchor not as a description to be completed but as a partial binary state across a set of structural dimensions — and let that partial state drive which domains the wandering agents are weighted toward, rather than trying to extract a 'full' anchor before the search begins.
source_cards.limit: Gagné's hierarchy is strictly deterministic and unidirectional: there is exactly one valid prerequisite sequence, and the system's entire function is to enforce it. That is structurally opposed to how your wandering logic must work — the anchor scores finds after the fact, it does not direct the path before it. Inheritance coding also operates entirely within a pre-defined skill tree; it has no mechanism for detecting resonance across unrelated domains, which is precisely what your agents are built to do. The bridge holds for the idea that a partial state can be a diagnostic surface, but it breaks completely at the question of how that surface should be used: Gagné collapses the search toward the already-known, your system must remain open to the structurally unexpected.

source_cards.spark: An AI that must infer the laws of physics from a handful of expensive measurements — never allowed to just observe everything — is solving the same structural problem as a system that must find what's most relevant to a concept without being told what to look for.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's anchor works the same way a MaD Physics budget works: it doesn't tell the wandering agent where to go, it just makes some directions more expensive than others by scoring resonance softly rather than directing the path. The agent's partial traversal of a domain — never complete, always constrained — is structurally identical to the MaD Physics agent's sparse measurement set: both are incomplete by design, and that incompleteness is what keeps the possibility space open long enough to find something genuinely surprising. The 'altered laws' design choice maps onto your inheritance-vs-transcendence tension: MaD Physics deliberately breaks known physics so the agent cannot retrieve a memorized answer, which is exactly the condition your wandering agents need to satisfy — the find must be a genuine structural resonance, not a cached association. And just as a MaD Physics agent that over-commits to early measurements collapses the inference space prematurely, a wandering agent that locks onto the anchor's most obvious surface meaning will stop finding the non-obvious bridges.
source_cards.source_shape: MaD Physics is a benchmark where an agent studies a physical system operating under deliberately altered laws — laws the agent cannot have memorized — and must figure out those laws using only a strict budget of measurements. Each measurement costs something, so the agent cannot simply observe until certainty arrives; it must choose which observations will unlock the most inference from the least data. The design forces the agent to work from a partial skeleton of evidence and reason forward, rather than pattern-match against prior knowledge.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your wandering agents have an explicit 'over-specification brake' — a signal that fires when the agent's path is converging too tightly on the anchor's literal terms too early, analogous to the MaD Physics budget that forces the agent to stay in the inferential space longer before committing.
source_cards.limit: MaD Physics is a convergent system with a finish line: the agent's incompleteness is a temporary constraint it is actively trying to eliminate, and success means closing the gap to a single correct answer. Your system treats vagueness as a permanent structural feature to be preserved, not a problem to be solved — the anchor is never meant to be fully resolved into a definitive match. MaD Physics also operates entirely within a single domain with a known target structure; your wandering agents cross unconstrained domain space looking for structural resonance in theater, biology, or wherever the wander leads, and MaD Physics has no mechanism that corresponds to that cross-domain traversal at all.

selection.why_these_cards: Gagné inheritance coding (P01-001) converts a partial binary state into a precise diagnostic ADDRESS — it tells you exactly which gap blocks forward movement. MaD Physics (S43d63a) forces an agent to spend a measurement budget where it yields the most inferential leverage. P01-001 is deterministic and backward-looking; S43d63a is exploratory and forward-acting. The tension is gap-as-known-blocker versus probe-as-uncertainty-collapser.
selection.spark: Gagné's gap says 'here is exactly what you are missing.' MaD Physics says 'spend your budget where it collapses the most uncertainty.' Collide them: what if the anchor's gaps are not deficits to fill but a budget allocation map telling the wander where to spend its limited traversal?
selection.motive: The cushion needs the system to know WHERE to wander when the user has only vague inspirations. This blend turns the structure of the user's vagueness into a directional weighting for the search without over-specifying it.
selection.tension: Gagné treats the gap as a single correct missing prerequisite to be filled deterministically; MaD Physics treats incompleteness as something to be probed by choosing high-leverage moves under a budget, with no single right next step. One closes the gap; the other exploits it.

blends.thesis: Anchor gaps as a probe-budget allocator: the unspecified regions of a user's half-formed concept can be read as a diagnostic address (Gagné) that is then converted into a measurement-leverage weighting (MaD) — so the wander spends most of its finite traversal on the exact structural joints the anchor left open, because those joints are where a find yields maximum inferential leverage about the user's emerging vision.
blends.mechanism: Represent the anchor as a partial binary state across structural dimensions. Unfilled dimensions are not errors — each is a coordinate. For each unfilled coordinate, compute expected inferential leverage: how much would a resonant find here reduce uncertainty about the whole shape? Weight the wandering agents' domain selection by that leverage score, not by similarity to filled dimensions. The wander spends its budget on open joints, not on confirming what's already there.
blends.emergent_structure: A leverage-weighted gap map: Gagné never ranks gaps by inferential value (its gap is just the blocking prerequisite), and MaD Physics never reads a SEMANTIC structure of pre-known gaps (it probes a closed physical law). The blend produces a ranking of where to wander derived from which of the user's OWN unspoken regions are highest-yield — present in neither source.
blends.advances_cushion: Gives the system a concrete answer to 'how do I find what to look for when the user only has vague inspirations' — direct the limited wander toward the user's most generative blind spots rather than toward what they already articulated, which is precisely the 'I can't articulate it yet' case.

source_cards.spark: Fuzzy logic treats a half-understood concept not as a broken version of full understanding, but as its own valid signal — and your system's ability to recognize a partial match without forcing it into a hard category is structurally the same move.
source_cards.bridge: Your wandering agents face this exact structure when they evaluate resonance between an external source and the user's anchor. The anchor is not a checklist — it's a shape, and a source can partially match that shape in ways that are genuinely informative rather than merely insufficient. When the agent scores a match at medium confidence across some mechanism nodes but not others, that partial score is not a failed full match; it is a structurally distinct signal pointing at which facets of the anchor the source illuminates. Fuzzy logic's core move — treating a 0.6 membership as real information rather than a rounding error — is exactly what makes your soft anchor scoring work: the incompleteness of the match is itself the signal, because a perfect match would mean the source is the anchor, not a new angle on it.
source_cards.source_shape: In fuzzy logic assessment, a student's knowledge isn't scored as right or wrong — it's assigned a membership value on a continuous scale, say 0.6 in 'understands fractions,' which is a real, distinct piece of information rather than a degraded version of 1.0. The system runs inference on these partial memberships directly: it doesn't wait for the student to reach full understanding before it can say something meaningful about where they are. Incompleteness is the input, not the obstacle.
source_cards.use: Consider making the partial match pattern explicit in the card — not just the confidence level, but which layers matched and which didn't, so the user sees the shape of the resonance rather than a single scalar. The gaps in the match are as informative as the hits.
source_cards.limit: The fuzzy logic in this paper is a closed system: domain experts pre-define the membership functions before any student is assessed, so the fuzziness is bounded and designed in advance. Your wandering system requires the opposite — the skeleton must stay open enough to recognize resonance in domains the user never named, which means the 'shape of acceptable partial knowledge' cannot be specified ahead of time. The bridge holds for the principle that incompleteness is a valid input rather than a deficiency, but breaks at the question of how that incompleteness is bounded: fuzzy assessment knows in advance what it's being partial about; your agents do not.

source_cards.spark: A paper on broken mathematical understanding accidentally named something true about your system: an incomplete structure has more connective surface than a finished one, because completion seals the joints that transfer requires.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor works the same way. A half-formed concept — three points of triangulation rather than a fully articulated thesis — preserves the structural joints that allow a wandering agent to find resonance in foreign domains. If the anchor were fully specified, those joints would be fused shut: the concept would be self-contained and nothing outside its own domain would 'fit.' The paper's unit-relationships are your anchor's triangulation points — the minimum load-bearing connectors that hold the shape without filling it in. And just as the paper found that transfer across problem types runs through those exposed joints rather than through the completed surface, your agents' best finds will attach to what the anchor has NOT yet said, not to what it has.
source_cards.source_shape: The paper studies students who partially understand mathematical concepts — not to grade them, but to map which structural relationships between units survive despite the gaps. Its key finding is that unit-relationships (the connective tissue between concepts) are more diagnostic than the concepts themselves: a student's incomplete competency profile reveals the underlying skeleton of their understanding more clearly than a complete answer would, because the joints are still exposed. Completeness hides structure; incompleteness shows it.
source_cards.use: Consider making productive incompleteness an explicit design principle for how users submit anchors — not a flaw to fix but a lever to preserve. A prompt that asks 'what are three things this is, and one thing it is not yet' might deliberately keep the structural joints open longer than a prompt that pushes toward full articulation.
source_cards.limit: This paper is diagnostic and retrospective: it measures incompleteness as a deficit that already happened and maps the resulting damage. Your system treats incompleteness as a live generative signal — not a wound to assess but a surface to match against. Those are opposite stances toward the same structural fact. The paper also never leaves its domain: it has no wandering logic, no mechanism for scoring finds from foreign territory, and no interest in cross-domain transfer. The bridge holds for the shape of incompleteness-as-connective-surface, but breaks entirely at the question of how to use that surface to reach outward.

source_cards.spark: A system that breathes life into static textbook diagrams by treating their incompleteness as a feature — not a flaw to fix — uses the same structural logic as a system that finds unexpected connections from a half-formed idea.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor — the user's half-formed concept or in-progress work — plays the role of the partially-parsed diagram: an incomplete structure that is rich enough to act as a match surface precisely because it has not been fully resolved. Just as Augmented Physics uses the diagram's skeleton to locate the right simulation rather than requiring a full specification up front, your system uses the anchor's incompleteness to find resonances that a fully-specified query would never surface. The 'inheritance vs. transcendence' tension maps directly: the system must honor what the user has already built or thought (inheritance) while returning connections that genuinely extend beyond it (transcendence) — collapsing into pure retrieval kills the value, but drifting so far that the anchor is abandoned kills relevance. The productive zone is the same in both systems: close enough to the original structure to be grounded, far enough from it to be generative.
source_cards.source_shape: Augmented Physics takes a printed diagram — frozen, finished, never meant to move — and extracts its partial structure using computer vision to generate a live, interactive simulation. The key is that the pipeline does not require a complete formal specification of the physics: a partially-parsed skeleton of the diagram is enough to trigger simulation generation. The system recognizes the diagram's shape rather than rebuilding it from scratch, and it honors the original artifact while producing something the original authors never built.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your anchor representation explicitly preserves and protects its unresolved regions — if the system is ever tempted to 'complete' or sharpen the anchor before searching, that is the moment the match surface shrinks and the most surprising connections become unreachable.
source_cards.limit: Augmented Physics operates inside a closed, deterministic domain: once the diagram is parsed, physical law tightly constrains what the simulation can produce. The incompleteness there is a temporary parsing problem — the goal is to resolve it into a correct simulation. Your system's incompleteness is the opposite: it must remain permanently unresolved, because the value of wandering collapses the moment the search space is pre-bounded by what the user already knows to name. The bridge holds for the 'skeleton-as-match-surface' shape, but breaks at the question of what incompleteness is for — in Augmented Physics it is an obstacle to overcome, in your system it is the mechanism itself.

selection.why_these_cards: Fuzzy logic (S195150) says a partial match is a distinct signal showing which facets matched. Sb4b50f-001 says incompleteness exposes the connective JOINTS that completion seals shut. Augmented Physics (P05-001) says a partial skeleton is enough to drive a live generation. Together they pull three ways: fuzzy=which facets, joints=which connectors, augmented=enough-to-animate. The friction is between describing a partial match and ACTING on it to generate.
selection.spark: Fuzzy logic shows the SHAPE of a partial match; Sb4b50f says the joints (not the surface) carry transfer; Augmented Physics shows a skeleton can drive live simulation. Collide: the partial-match profile is not just a confidence report — it is itself a runnable skeleton.
selection.motive: The cushion needs the system to take a vague inspiration and 'make it live' — articulate and animate something the user couldn't. This blend gives a mechanism for turning the shape of a partial match into a generative scaffold.
selection.tension: Fuzzy logic and Sb4b50f are diagnostic/descriptive — they tell you what matched and where the joints are, but stop. Augmented Physics is generative — it animates. Diagnosis versus animation: the descriptive cards have no engine, the generative card has no semantics of partial match.

blends.thesis: The partial-match profile is a runnable articulation skeleton: when a found source matches the anchor on some facets and not others (fuzzy), the UNMATCHED facets are exposed joints (Sb4b50f) that mark exactly where new structure must be generated — and that joint-map is complete enough to drive a live articulation of the user's vague concept (Augmented), turning 'I can't say what it is' into a generated draft of what it could be.
blends.mechanism: Score a find against the anchor facet-by-facet (fuzzy membership per node). The matched facets become inherited structure (the grounding). The unmatched facets are the open joints — the system does not discard them, it treats each open joint as a generation slot. An articulation engine then fills those slots by extrapolating from the matched-facet structure plus the found source, producing a concrete candidate articulation. The user reacts; their reaction re-weights which joints stay open.
blends.emergent_structure: Unmatched facets become generation slots rather than confidence deductions or mere diagnostic markers. Fuzzy logic treats a 0.6 as information but never generates from the 0.4 gap; Sb4b50f names the joints but never animates them; Augmented Physics animates but has no concept of a per-facet match gap. The blend makes the gap itself the generative input.
blends.advances_cushion: Directly attacks the 'I have vague inspirations but don't know how to articulate or make it live' case — the system uses what partially matched to inherit, and uses what DIDN'T match as the exact specification of what to generate next, producing a living draft instead of a similarity score.

source_cards.spark: A system that learns to recognize which mathematical conjectures are worth pursuing — not by checking their truth, but by scoring their interestingness — is doing structurally the same thing your wandering agents need to do: evaluate a find without being told what to look for.
source_cards.bridge: Your wandering agents face the same structural problem: they need a way to recognize that a find is relevant to the user's anchor without being given a checklist of what relevance looks like. The paper's interestingness signal maps directly onto what your system calls the soft anchor — a scoring layer that can say 'this is close to something that matters' without specifying the path the agent should have taken to get there. Just as the paper separates generation from evaluation (conjecture first, score second), your architecture separates wandering from judgment: the agent drifts freely, and the anchor's pull is felt at the scoring stage, not the traversal stage. The correspondence holds at the mechanism level: both systems need a signal that is evaluative without being directive, because the moment the signal starts directing the search, it stops finding anything the user didn't already know to look for.
source_cards.source_shape: The paper trains a model to predict whether a mathematical conjecture is 'interesting' — meaning worth a human mathematician's attention — before anyone has proved or disproved it. The model learns this signal from a corpus of prior mathematical work, then uses it to steer automated theory formation: candidates get generated freely, and the interestingness score decides which ones survive into further exploration. The key move is that the scoring function never dictates what gets generated — it only judges what has already appeared, leaving the generative process unconstrained.
source_cards.use: Consider examining how the paper operationalizes its interestingness signal — specifically, what features it scores on and how it avoids collapsing toward a single attractor — as a design reference for how your anchor scoring function could be structured to reward resonance without rewarding familiarity.
source_cards.limit: The paper's interestingness signal is trained on a corpus of prior mathematical work, which means it learns to recognize what human mathematicians have historically found valuable. That is the opposite of what your anchor is. Your anchor is the user's half-formed, pre-articulate vision — by design, it sits outside existing categories and may point toward something no prior corpus would score highly. A scoring function trained on historical preference will systematically undervalue exactly the finds that matter most to your users. The bridge holds for the architectural shape — evaluate without directing — but breaks at the question of what the scoring function is trained on and toward: the paper's signal converges on the known-interesting, where yours must remain open to the structurally novel.

source_cards.spark: A construct map in mathematics education defines a progression space precisely enough to recognize where a learner is — without ever prescribing the path they must take to get there. That separation between 'rich enough to recognize' and 'loose enough to permit varied traversal' is the same structural tension your system is trying to hold.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor plays the role of the construct map: it needs to be rich enough that a wandering agent can recognize when something resonates with it, but loose enough that it doesn't collapse the traversal space by over-specifying what the user is looking for. The construct map's insistence that the skeleton be validated before it scores anything maps directly onto your system's claim that productive incompleteness is the structural property the anchor must preserve — a fully articulated anchor is like a fully articulated construct map: it stops being a recognition device and becomes a filter that only confirms what was already known. The paper's phrase 'the map precedes the measurement' is the key point of contact: the anchor must exist as a stable-but-open shape before any agent traversal happens, or the agents have nothing to triangulate against. Where the analogy holds tightest is here: both systems require the skeleton to be stable enough to score without being complete enough to direct.
source_cards.source_shape: A construct map is a validated skeleton of a proficiency domain: it defines the shape of advancement — what counts as more sophisticated, what counts as less — without specifying which route any individual student must travel. Crucially, the map must be validated as a skeleton first, before it can score anything. It scores traversal after the fact; it never directs it. The map is incomplete by design — complete enough to recognize resonance when a student's work lands somewhere on it, incomplete enough that many different paths through the domain all register as valid.
source_cards.use: Consider stress-testing your anchor's current articulation against this criterion: if you handed it to a wandering agent, would the agent be able to recognize a resonant hit without the anchor having told it what domain to enter or what form the hit should take? If the anchor is already directing rather than recognizing, it may be over-specified — and this paper's construct-map validation methodology might offer a concrete procedure for checking that.
source_cards.limit: The construct map is explicitly designed to become complete over time — its entire purpose is to fully articulate a proficiency continuum as evidence accumulates. That goal is structurally opposite to what your anchor mechanism requires: full articulation narrows the match surface toward zero, which is the failure mode your system is built to avoid. Beyond that, the construct map operates in a single predetermined domain with a fixed outcome space and a known population of learners; your wandering agents are defined precisely by their freedom to enter unnamed, unconstrained domains. The paper's instrument is domain-locked by design; your anchor's value depends on being domain-unlocked. The bridge holds for the recognition-without-direction shape, but breaks entirely at the question of completeness as a goal and domain-boundedness as a feature.

source_cards.spark: A system that learns what counts as 'interesting' — without being told in advance — is structurally the same problem as a system that recognizes resonance with a user's anchor without being told what to look for.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor mechanism faces the same structural knot: the user's concept is often vague, and that vagueness is not a problem to be resolved before the wander begins — it is the live scoring schema the wander runs against. FERMAT operationalizes exactly this: it does not pre-specify interestingness, it evolves the measure, which maps directly to the principle that the anchor should remain productively incomplete rather than be sharpened into a hard filter. The 'soft anchor scores without directing' node in your system is the functional equivalent of FERMAT's learned interestingness function — both must be loose enough to permit genuine discovery, rich enough to reject noise. And just as FERMAT's evolutionary synthesis of measures is what keeps the system from collapsing into retrieval of the already-known, your anchor's incompleteness is what keeps the wandering agents from just returning obvious matches.
source_cards.source_shape: FERMAT is a reinforcement learning environment for open-ended mathematical discovery. Its central challenge is that you cannot hand the system a fixed reward signal when the goal is genuine novelty — a fixed reward just finds more of what you already know. So FERMAT instead evolves the scoring function itself: it uses evolutionary algorithms and LLMs to synthesize 'interestingness measures,' testing and refining what counts as worth pursuing as it goes. The result is a system that holds its own evaluation criteria loosely enough to be surprised, yet tightly enough to distinguish a real find from noise.
source_cards.use: Consider whether the mechanism you use to score resonance between a source and the anchor could itself be treated as an evolvable or learnable object — not a fixed rubric, but something that updates as the user's concept becomes clearer through the cards they accept or reject.
source_cards.limit: FERMAT is entirely domain-internal: its interestingness measures are defined over mathematical objects with symbolic structure, and the system has no mechanism for recognizing that a result in one domain is structurally similar to a result in another. Constellax's load-bearing requirement is the opposite — wandering agents must detect structural resonance across theater, physics, literature, and mathematics simultaneously. FERMAT's scoring machinery would need fundamental architectural extension, not scaling, to cross domain boundaries; the bridge holds for the 'learn what counts as worth pursuing' shape, but breaks at the question of how that judgment transfers across incompatible symbol systems.

selection.why_these_cards: P01-003's interestingness signal is trained on a HISTORICAL corpus — it converges on the known-interesting, which is fatal for novelty. The construct map (P01-002) is validated before it scores and is designed to become COMPLETE over time. FERMAT-evolved measures (S413861) evolve the scorer DURING search to stay surprisable. Three scorers, three opposed temporal stances: trained-on-past, validated-then-completing, evolving-forward. Maximum tension on the question 'what is the scorer anchored to in time?'
selection.spark: All three are scoring functions, but P01-003 looks backward (corpus), P01-002 aims to complete forward into a fixed map, and S413861 evolves to stay open. The cushion's user explicitly wants to NOT stay at the same level as the prior work — so a backward-trained scorer is poison. What scorer transcends its own training corpus?
selection.motive: The cushion's defining ambition — 'I don't want to be at the same level, I want to take it to the next level' — is exactly the failure mode P01-003 warns about: a scorer trained on the known undervalues the transcendent. This blend constructs a scorer that uses the prior corpus as a baseline to exceed, not a target to match.
selection.tension: P01-003 rewards resemblance to historically-valued work (anchors to the past). S413861 evolves to escape the already-known (anchors to the search frontier). P01-002 wants the map to eventually become complete (anchors to a fixed future endpoint). No two agree on what the score should converge toward.

blends.thesis: A transcendence-scored anchor: the prior published work is loaded not as a positive target (P01-003's mistake) but as a baseline the system scores finds for EXCEEDING — a find earns relevance by sharing the anchor's structural joints (construct-map recognition) while scoring HIGH on an evolving novelty-against-the-corpus measure (FERMAT), so the scorer's reward gradient points away from the inherited work even as it stays anchored to its skeleton.
blends.mechanism: Two-component score per find: (1) recognition — does it land on the anchor's validated skeleton (construct-map style, scores after the fact, never directs)? Gates relevance. (2) transcendence — an evolving measure that scores the find by structural DISTANCE from the prior corpus along the anchor's own joints, where the measure updates as the user accepts/rejects cards (FERMAT-evolved). Finds that recognize the skeleton AND maximize corpus-distance score highest. The construct map provides the 'still about the same thing' constraint; the evolving measure provides the 'but beyond it' gradient.
blends.emergent_structure: A scorer whose reward gradient points AWAY from its anchoring corpus — neither source has this. P01-003 rewards corpus-proximity, P01-002 rewards convergence to a fixed complete map, S413861 evolves but has no corpus-baseline to push off of. The blend makes the inherited work a repulsive baseline along an attractive skeleton.
blends.advances_cushion: This is the literal mechanism for the user's stated goal: take a published method/research and go to the NEXT level, not the same level. It operationalizes 'inheritance without staying at the same level' as a two-component score — inherit the joints, repel from the surface.
